To calculate the probability that a randomly selected student from Group B has a higher engagement level than a randomly selected student from Group A we consider the difference in their engagement levels. Let $ D = X_B - X_A $. The distribution of $ D $ is also normal because the difference of two independent normal variables is normal. The mean of $ D $ is $ \mu_D = \mu_B - \mu_A = 75 - 70 = 5 $. The variance of $ D $ is $ \sigma_D^2 = \sigma_B^2 + \sigma_A^2 = 12^2 + 10^2 = 144 + 100 = 244 $. Therefore the standard deviation of $ D $ is $ \sigma_D = \sqrt{244} \approx 15.6205 $.

We want to find $ P(D > 0) $ which is the same as $ P(Z > \frac{0 - \mu_D}{\sigma_D}) = P(Z > \frac{-5}{15.6205}) = P(Z > -0.3199) $. Looking up this value in the standard normal distribution table we find that the probability is approximately 0.6255. Hence the probability that a randomly selected student from Group B has a higher engagement level than a randomly selected student from Group A is approximately 0.6255 or 62.55 percent.

Now we move on to the second part of the problem. The performance improvement of Group A is modeled by an exponential growth function $ P_A(t) = 50 e^{0.02 t} $. The performance improvement of Group B is modeled by a logistic growth model $ P_B(t) = \frac{100}{1 + e^{-0.15(t - 5)}} $. We are to find the time $ t $ at which both groups have the same performance improvement level. This requires solving the equation $ 50 e^{0.02 t} = \frac{100}{1 + e^{-0.15(t - 5)}} $. 

The explanation for solving this equation involves understanding the nature of both models. The exponential growth function increases continuously while the logistic function increases rapidly at first then levels off.
